history.topic: test 3
history.script: A big titty goth girl stands on her porch at night. A single crow flies over and drops a key into her hand. She rewards it with a snack. The key is the key to the bank, which the big titty goth girl is planning on robbing later this afternoon for profit....

history.topic: test 2
test
history.script: the woman walks over to the door nude. She welcomes the pizza driver in. The pizza driver looks at her nude body and small breasts.

history.topic: why do goth chicks have big boobs
why is princess peach so hot?
why the 2000s were the best time
history.style_notes: add "2000's" to all the image prompts

history.topic: Neuroscientist Lisa Feldman Barrett's theory of constructed emotion, detailed in her 2017 book How Emotions Are Made, proposes that emotions are not universal hardwired reactions that the brain detects and labels but active predictions the brain constructs based on past experience, body budget, and cultural learning — which means that the same physiological state of elevated heart rate and shallow breathing can be constructed as anxiety, excitement, or anticipation depending on context; explain what the theory of constructed emotion proposes and how it differs from the basic emotions model associated with Paul Ekman, what the implications are for anxiety specifically and why Barrett argues that people have more agency over their emotional experience than they believe, and what this means for why meditation practices that train interoceptive awareness and cognitive reappraisal work at the level of emotion construction rather than emotion suppression

Havening is a psychosensory technique developed by neuroscientist Ronald Ruden that involves applying gentle touch to specific areas of the body — the face, upper arms, and palms — while simultaneously engaging in eye movements and positive visualization, with the claimed mechanism being that the touch generates delta waves in the amygdala that disrupt the electrochemical encoding of traumatic memories; explain what Havening is and what the neuroscience it draws on claims, what the evidence base currently looks like including what it shares with EMDR and where it diverges, why delta wave generation in the amygdala through sensory input is a plausible but not yet well-established mechanism, and why practices involving gentle self-touch are increasingly studied as accessible self-regulation tools regardless of the specific theoretical framework

A 2015 paper in Psychological Science by researchers Benjamin Baird and Jonathan Schooler found that mind-wandering — the default mode network's spontaneous self-generated thought — is the brain state most associated with creative insight, and that attempts to suppress or focus away from it during incubation periods for creative problems actually reduced the quality of subsequent solutions; explain what the incubation effect is in creativity research, why the default mode network is now understood as the brain's creative workshop rather than its idle mode, what this means for the relationship between meditation — which trains the ability to both enter and exit mind-wandering deliberately — and creative output, and why some researchers argue that the most productive meditative state for creative professionals is not focused concentration but trained non-directive awareness

Dialectical behavior therapy, developed by psychologist Marsha Linehan at the University of Washington partly drawing on her own experience with borderline personality disorder, contains a set of crisis survival skills called TIPP — Temperature, Intense exercise, Paced breathing, and Paired muscle relaxation — in which the Temperature skill involves submerging the face in cold water to trigger the mammalian dive reflex, which forcibly slows heart rate; explain what the mammalian dive reflex is and what triggers it, what the TIPP skill set is and what population it was designed for, why the dive reflex is one of the fastest available methods to interrupt acute emotional dysregulation, and what this shares mechanistically with the cold exposure component of practices like the Wim Hof method

A 2021 study published in Nature Communications by researchers at Trinity College Dublin found that breathwork improves attention and cognitive performance because the act of breathing directly modulates the firing rate of the locus coeruleus — the brain's primary noradrenaline center and the region most responsible for attention regulation; explain what the locus coeruleus is and why it is considered the brain's arousal and attention hub, what the specific finding was about how the inhale phase versus the exhale phase produces different cognitive states, why this research provides a neural explanation for why meditators recommend breathing awareness as the primary anchor for attention training, and what the practical implication is for anyone who wants to sharpen focus before a demanding task

The Satyananda Yoga Nidra system, codified by Swami Satyananda Saraswati at the Bihar School of Yoga in the 1960s drawing on the ancient Tantric text the Mandukya Upanishad, is the specific lineage that most modern Yoga Nidra protocols including NSDR trace back to; explain what distinguishes Satyananda Yoga Nidra from other relaxation practices including its use of sankalpa or resolve, the rotation of awareness through 61 body points in a specific sequence based on the motor homunculus, the use of pairs of opposite sensations to destabilize fixed mental patterns, and the visualization stage drawn from Tantric imagery — and explain what the research from the Indian Defence Institute of Physiology and Allied Sciences shows about its measurable effects on stress hormones in soldiers

Psychophysiological insomnia — the most common clinical form of insomnia affecting an estimated 15 percent of adults — is not a sleep disorder in the traditional sense but a conditioned arousal response in which the bed itself has become a trigger for wakefulness through repeated association with the frustration of not sleeping; explain what psychophysiological insomnia is and how it develops through classical conditioning, why this is why sleep restriction therapy — the counterintuitive CBT-I technique of deliberately limiting time in bed — is the most effective behavioral treatment for chronic insomnia with remission rates exceeding antidepressants and sleep medications in head-to-head trials, and what stimulus control therapy is and why removing screens and anxiety-provoking activities from the bedroom environment is a neurological intervention not just a hygiene suggestion

Non-directive meditation — a category that includes Norwegian Acem meditation and certain forms of Zen — explicitly instructs practitioners not to concentrate, not to observe the breath, and not to attempt to control mental content, but simply to sit and allow thoughts to arise and pass without engagement; explain what non-directive meditation is and how it differs from both focused attention and open monitoring practices, what a 2016 study from the Norwegian University of Science and Technology found when comparing non-directive meditation to concentrative practices using fMRI — specifically its finding that non-directive practice produced significantly higher default mode network activity, which is counterintuitive — and why the researchers interpreted this as evidence of a more thorough form of mental processing rather than distraction

The concept of ego dissolution — the temporary loss of the boundary between self and environment — occurs on a spectrum from the mild boundary softening of deep meditation to the complete self-dissolution reported in psychedelic states, and neuroscientist Robin Carhart-Harris at UCSF has mapped its neural correlates using fMRI to show that it corresponds precisely to a reduction in default mode network activity; explain what ego dissolution is, why the default mode network is understood as the neural basis of the narrative self, what the research shows about why its quieting produces feelings of unity and expanded awareness, and why meditation traditions have described this experience for three thousand years without the neuroimaging vocabulary to explain the mechanism

The Wim Hof method combines three distinct practices — a specific hyperventilation-based breathwork protocol, cold exposure, and commitment training — and was brought to scientific attention when Hof demonstrated voluntary control over his autonomic immune response in a 2014 Radboud University study in which participants trained in his method showed significantly attenuated immune responses to an injected endotoxin compared to controls; explain what the Wim Hof method actually consists of, what the specific physiological mechanism of the breathing protocol is including its effect on blood CO2 levels and adrenaline release, what the Radboud study showed and what it did not show, and why the method sits at the intersection of legitimate physiological research and significant overclaiming

Researcher Kristin Neff at the University of Texas Austin has produced over two decades of research showing that self-compassion — treating yourself with the same care you would offer a friend — is a stronger predictor of emotional resilience, motivation, and mental health than self-esteem, which is contingent on performance; explain what self-compassion is and what its three components are according to Neff's model including self-kindness, common humanity, and mindfulness, why self-esteem is an unstable psychological resource that collapses precisely when it is most needed, and what the neuroscience shows about the distinct brain activation patterns of self-criticism versus self-compassion and why the difference matters for sleep and anxiety

Tonglen is a Tibetan Buddhist meditation practice that deliberately reverses the usual self-protective instinct — instead of breathing in clean air and breathing out toxicity, the practitioner visualizes breathing in the suffering of others and breathing out relief; explain what Tonglen is and what the psychological mechanism is behind deliberately moving toward difficulty rather than away from it, how Pema Chödrön modernized the practice for Western audiences and what she identifies as its primary function in working with personal fear and aversion, and what the compassion fatigue research shows about why this counterintuitive direction of practice may build resilience rather than drain it

A landmark 2003 study by psychologist Sheldon Cohen at Carnegie Mellon University exposed 334 volunteers to the common cold virus after assessing their sleep duration — people sleeping under seven hours were nearly three times more likely to develop a cold than those sleeping eight hours or more, and the effect was dose-dependent; explain what sleep does to the immune system at the cellular level including its effect on cytokine production and natural killer cell deployment, why this research is considered some of the cleanest causal evidence linking sleep and immune function, and what it means practically for people who cut sleep during high-stress periods when immune function matters most

The body doubling effect is a productivity and nervous system phenomenon in which the mere presence of another person — not their input, not their feedback, just their physical or virtual presence — measurably improves focus and task completion in people with ADHD and anxiety; explain what body doubling is, what the proposed neurological mechanisms are including co-regulation through the social engagement system described in Stephen Porges's polyvagal theory, why this is why meditation in a group setting produces measurably deeper relaxation than solo practice for most beginners, and how this principle underlies the ancient monastic tradition of communal silent sitting

Neuroscientist Matthew Walker's research at UC Berkeley found that pulling an all-nighter produces a 40 percent deficit in the prefrontal cortex's ability to form new memories — not a minor impairment but a near-total shutdown of the brain's primary learning region; explain exactly what happens to hippocampal memory encoding during sleep deprivation, why the feeling of having adapted to less sleep is itself a symptom of impairment rather than evidence of resilience, and why Walker argues that no aspect of human biology degrades as steeply or as rapidly under sleep loss as cognitive performance — frame this as a corrective to the cultural glorification of running on five hours

The use of Black patients as non-consenting subjects in American medical research was not exceptional — it was systematic. J. Marion Sims, celebrated as the father of modern gynaecology, developed his surgical techniques for repairing obstetric fistulas through repeated operations on enslaved Black women in Alabama in the 1840s without anaesthesia, which was available, on the stated premise that Black people experienced pain differently. His statue stood in Central Park until 2018. Medical schools in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries routinely obtained Black cadavers through grave-robbing for anatomical instruction when legal access to bodies was restricted. Documented evidence from multiple Southern medical schools confirms the practice. The Tuskegee study ran for forty years. The Guatemala experiments were conducted on the same population as Tuskegee by the same lead researcher during the same period. HeLa cells were commercialised for seventy years. These are not isolated incidents in the record. They are the record.

Beginning in the early 1980s and running through most of the decade, American prosecutors secured convictions in dozens of child sexual abuse cases at daycare centres and preschools based almost entirely on the testimony of very young children who had been subjected to highly suggestive interviewing techniques that researchers in child psychology had already identified as producing false memories and confabulated accounts. The McMartin preschool trial in California became the longest and most expensive criminal trial in American history at the time, resulting in no convictions. Others were not so fortunate. Kelly Michaels was convicted in New Jersey and served five years before her conviction was overturned. The Amirault family in Massachusetts served years in prison. The Kniffen and McCuan families in California served fourteen years each before being released. The interviewing methodologies used to obtain the children's testimony were subsequently banned in most jurisdictions. The prosecutors who secured the convictions faced no professional consequences.

In the months surrounding Israel's declaration of independence in May 1948, approximately 700,000 Palestinian Arabs — roughly half the Arab population of Mandatory Palestine — fled or were expelled from their homes. Villages were depopulated and in many cases demolished. The events are documented in Israeli military archives, British Mandate records, and the testimony of participants on all sides. Israeli historian Benny Morris, drawing on declassified Israeli Defence Forces documents in the 1980s, confirmed that expulsions were carried out deliberately in numerous locations as military policy, not solely as a consequence of the fighting or of Arab leaders' instructions to flee. The Palestinian Arabic term for these events is the Nakba — the catastrophe. In 2009 the Israeli Education Ministry removed the word from Arab-Israeli school textbooks. A 2011 Israeli law allows the government to withhold funding from institutions that hold public commemorations of the Nakba. The 700,000 who left did not return. Their descendants, now numbering in the millions, remain in refugee status.

In 1994 a paralegal working for a law firm representing a tobacco company's litigation mailed a box of internal corporate documents to anti-tobacco researcher Stanton Glantz at the University of California San Francisco. The documents, which came to be known as the Cigarette Papers, included internal research from Brown and Williamson dating to the 1960s that showed company scientists had concluded privately that nicotine was addictive and that smoking caused cancer — findings the company's public representatives were simultaneously denying under oath before Congress. One 1963 memo from the company's general counsel stated explicitly that the company was, in the business of selling nicotine, an addictive drug. Executives of all seven major American tobacco companies testified before Congress in April 1994 that they did not believe nicotine was addictive. The documents had already been written. The paralegal who leaked them was identified, dismissed, and sued. A federal settlement with the tobacco industry was reached in 1998. No executive was ever charged with perjury.

The 1918 influenza pandemic killed between 50 and 100 million people worldwide — more than died in the First World War. It is called the Spanish Flu because Spain, which was neutral and had no wartime press censorship, freely reported on its outbreak. The countries where it most likely originated and where it spread first — including the United States, where early cases were documented at Camp Funston in Kansas — suppressed reporting on it as bad for wartime morale. The name Spanish Flu was the result of one country being honest while the others hid what was happening. The wartime suppression of information about the pandemic's severity almost certainly accelerated its spread. Military demobilisation movements at the end of the war in 1918 transported it globally in weeks. The country that named it had the fewest early cases of any major nation. This is documented in the wartime press records of every country involved.

On May 13 1985 the Philadelphia Police Department dropped a military-grade explosive device from a state police helicopter onto a row house in a residential neighbourhood of the city. The target was the headquarters of MOVE, a Black liberation organisation that had been in a prolonged conflict with city authorities. The bomb ignited a fire that the fire department was ordered not to extinguish. The fire burned eleven houses, killed eleven people including five children, and left 250 people homeless. The police commissioner and the managing director both confirmed the decision to let the fire burn had been made deliberately. The city's Managing Director later testified he had made the call. No criminal charges were filed against any city official. The city eventually settled civil suits. In 2021 a Philadelphia grand jury found that city officials had committed criminal acts but that the statute of limitations had expired. The neighbourhood was rebuilt with substandard materials. Many residents sued again.

In December 1946 a US military tribunal at Nuremberg convicted seven Nazi physicians of war crimes and crimes against humanity for conducting medical experiments on concentration camp prisoners without consent. Seven were hanged. The trial produced a document called the Nuremberg Code — ten principles establishing the ethical requirements for human experimentation, the first of which states that voluntary consent is absolutely essential. The United States was the primary author of the Nuremberg Code. Within three years of the verdict, the United States Public Health Service had resumed the Tuskegee syphilis study — in which Black men with syphilis were deliberately left untreated without their knowledge — and launched the Guatemala syphilis experiments. The Nuremberg Code was never incorporated into US federal law. American researchers were aware of it and cited it in internal documents. Several chose not to apply it to their own work.

Both Ford Motor Company and General Motors maintained and operated subsidiary manufacturing plants in Germany throughout the Second World War that produced vehicles and equipment for the Nazi military. Opel, GM's German subsidiary, manufactured the Blitz truck that was the primary transport vehicle of the German Army. Ford's German subsidiary produced vehicles including the three-ton truck used extensively by the Wehrmacht. Both companies had taken steps before the war to Aryanise their German operations to comply with Nazi law. Internal corporate documents and postwar congressional investigations confirmed the relationship. After the war both companies filed — and received — compensation from the US government for wartime damage to their German plants, which had been bombed by Allied aircraft. Ford received approximately $1 million. GM received approximately $33 million. The compensation was paid from US Treasury funds.

The Endurance of Diego Garcia: An Entire Population Expelled for a Military Base Between 1968 and 1973 the British government deported the entire indigenous population of the Chagos Islands in the Indian Ocean — approximately 1,500 to 2,000 people known as the Chagossians — to make way for a US military base on the island of Diego Garcia. The deportation was carried out covertly. Classified British government documents obtained decades later show officials discussed how to deny the existence of a permanent population on the islands to avoid international scrutiny. In one document, officials suggested describing the islanders as "a few Tarzans" to minimise their legal standing. The Chagossians were deposited in Mauritius and the Seychelles with minimal resettlement support. British courts ruled their deportation illegal in 2000. The government used a royal prerogative order to override that ruling in 2004. Legal challenges have continued for fifty years. As of 2024, sovereignty negotiations over the islands are ongoing and the Chagossians have still not been permitted to return.

For most of the twentieth century the island of Nauru in the central Pacific was strip-mined for phosphate — one of the most valuable agricultural minerals on earth — by a consortium controlled by the British, Australian, and New Zealand governments. Nauru's phosphate made the farmland of three nations fertile for decades. When Nauru gained independence in 1968 and eventually took control of its own mining revenues in the 1970s, it briefly became one of the wealthiest nations per capita on earth. The money was mismanaged and the mining continued. By 2000 the phosphate was effectively exhausted and approximately 80 percent of the island's interior had been reduced to uninhabitable limestone pinnacles. The colonial mining period had left the island's population without the land base needed for food sovereignty. The governments that extracted the wealth paid royalties far below market value for decades. A renegotiated settlement was reached only after independence. The rehabilitation of the mined-out interior, if possible at all, has never been funded.

On January 17 1961 Dwight Eisenhower delivered his farewell address as President of the United States and warned explicitly of the danger posed by what he called the military-industrial complex — the permanent alliance between the defence industry and the military establishment that he said could acquire unwarranted influence over American government and policy. What is less often noted is that Eisenhower's original draft used the phrase "military-industrial-congressional complex." The word congressional was removed before delivery. Eisenhower had spent eight years as president watching defence contractors cultivate congressional relationships to protect contracts irrespective of military necessity. He had tried and failed to reduce the defence budget consistently against industry and congressional resistance. The speech was the valediction of a man who had seen the system from the inside and believed it was already beyond the control of elected government. The original draft with the deleted word is in the Eisenhower Presidential Library.

After the Civil War, Southern states rapidly expanded their criminal codes to criminalise behaviours — vagrancy, unemployment, minor debt — that applied almost exclusively to the newly freed Black population. Men convicted under these laws were leased to private corporations, farms, and mines as forced labour. They received no wages. They could not leave. They could be physically punished. The mortality rates in convict labour camps in Alabama, Mississippi, and Georgia in the 1880s and 1890s exceeded those in antebellum slavery because the leasing companies had no long-term financial interest in keeping workers alive. A lease was cheaper than a purchase. The system was documented by journalist Ida B. Wells and later by scholar Douglas Blackmon in research drawn directly from state government and corporate records. Convict leasing was not abolished in Alabama until 1928. It persisted in various legal forms in other states past World War II.

In the Soviet Union from the 1960s through the 1980s, psychiatrists working within the state system routinely diagnosed political dissidents with a condition called "sluggish schizophrenia" — a diagnosis invented by Soviet psychiatrist Andrei Snezhnevsky that did not exist in any international diagnostic framework. Its defining symptoms included, in practice, disagreement with Soviet ideology, religious belief, and the desire to emigrate. Patients diagnosed with it were committed to psychiatric hospitals called psikhushki, where they were administered antipsychotic drugs as punishment rather than treatment. The World Psychiatric Association formally condemned the practice in 1983 under international pressure. The Soviet delegation withdrew from the organisation before it could be expelled. The diagnosis of sluggish schizophrenia was used on at least a documented 800 political prisoners. The actual figure is believed to be significantly higher.

On the night of December 2 1984 a Union Carbide pesticide plant in Bhopal, India released 40 tonnes of methyl isocyanate gas into a city of 900,000 people. Between 15,000 and 25,000 people died — estimates vary because the Indian government stopped counting. Half a million survivors were registered for compensation. The average payout per survivor from the 1989 settlement was $500. Union Carbide's CEO Warren Anderson flew to India after the disaster, was briefly detained, posted bail, and left the country. India issued an arrest warrant for him in 1992. The United States never extradited him. He died in Florida in 2014. The Bhopal plant site was never fully cleaned up. Studies of groundwater near the facility published as recently as 2022 continue to find toxic contamination. Children born in the area since the disaster show statistically elevated rates of congenital defects.

Awe — the emotion produced by encountering something vast that requires an update to your mental framework, such as a starfield, a cathedral, or an overwhelming piece of music — has been shown in research from the University of California Berkeley to measurably reduce inflammatory cytokines, increase feelings of time abundance, and reduce self-referential thought more effectively than most positive emotions; explain what the neuroscience and psychology of awe shows, why awe specifically requires both vastness and accommodation rather than just beauty or pleasure, what Dacher Keltner's research shows about awe's unique position in the emotional landscape, and why cultivating deliberate awe experiences is increasingly studied as an accessible low-cost intervention for chronic stress and depression

Autogenic training is a self-regulation technique developed by German psychiatrist Johannes Schultz in the 1920s based on hypnotic research, in which the practitioner silently repeats a series of six standard phrases directing sensations of heaviness and warmth to specific body regions — explain what autogenic training is and how it differs from progressive muscle relaxation and body scan meditation, what the proposed mechanism is by which verbal self-suggestion produces measurable physiological changes in peripheral blood flow and muscle tension, what the clinical evidence base looks like across anxiety, insomnia, and psychosomatic conditions, and why it remains more widely used in European clinical settings than in North American ones

The average person has approximately six thousand thoughts per day according to a 2020 study from Queen's University that used a neural signature of thought transitions to count discrete thoughts for the first time — and roughly eighty percent of those thoughts are repetitions of thoughts from the previous day; explain what the research on thought frequency and repetition shows, what the default mode network's role is in generating repetitive self-referential thought, why the content of repeated thoughts is more often negative than positive due to the brain's negativity bias and its evolutionary function as a threat-prediction system, and what meditation research shows about the ability to reduce thought repetition over time

Sound bath meditation uses sustained resonant tones from instruments such as Tibetan singing bowls, crystal bowls, gongs, and tuning forks to create a bath of overlapping frequencies that the listener passively receives — explain what a sound bath is and what the proposed mechanisms are for its effects, what the difference is between the entrainment theory and the relaxation response theory of how sound baths work, what the current clinical research shows and does not show about measurable effects on cortisol and nervous system tone, and why the practice has seen significant clinical adoption in palliative care and cancer treatment settings despite limited randomized controlled trial evidence

Dreaming occurs primarily during REM sleep but also in brief flashes during NSDR and the hypnagogic threshold state, and the content of dreams is now understood to be functionally related to emotional memory consolidation rather than random neural noise; explain what Freud got wrong and what he got right about the function of dreams according to modern neuroscience, what Matthew Walker's research shows about the specific role of REM dreaming in processing emotionally charged memories, why people who are deprived of REM specifically show more reactive responses to negative emotional stimuli the following day, and what lucid dreaming research reveals about the degree of conscious access available during sleep

The Jacobson progressive relaxation method, developed by physician Edmund Jacobson beginning in the 1920s after forty years of clinical research, is one of the most thoroughly evidence-based behavioral interventions for anxiety and insomnia in the clinical literature — explain the full theoretical basis of why deliberately tensing a muscle before releasing it produces deeper relaxation than trying to relax directly, how the technique differs from simple body scanning, what the original clinical population was that Jacobson developed it for, and why it remains a first-line recommendation in cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia despite being nearly a century old

Time perception distorts measurably during meditation — experienced practitioners consistently underestimate the duration of a meditation session, sometimes by thirty to forty percent, which reflects a genuine change in how the brain processes time rather than inattention; explain what the neuroscience of time perception shows about its relationship to the default mode network and self-referential thought, why the feeling that time slows down during stress and speeds up during flow states are both accurate reflections of neural processing rate, and what the research on meditation and altered time perception reveals about the relationship between present-moment awareness and subjective time

Breathwork, as a broad category, contains fundamentally different physiological mechanisms depending on the technique — some activate the sympathetic nervous system deliberately, like holotropic breathing and the Wim Hof method, while others activate the parasympathetic, like 4-7-8 and coherent breathing; explain why these two categories of breathwork produce opposite nervous system states and opposite use cases, why activating the sympathetic system deliberately can build stress resilience over time, what the difference is between stress inoculation breathwork and calming breathwork, and why combining the two types in the same session requires careful sequencing

he glymphatic system, which clears toxic waste from the brain during sleep, is approximately sixty percent more active when you sleep on your side than on your back — a finding from research at the University of Rochester that used MRI to track cerebrospinal fluid flow in different sleep positions; explain what sleep position has to do with brain waste clearance, why the lateral position appears to be the most efficient for glymphatic drainage, what researchers speculate about the evolutionary origins of side sleeping as the dominant position across most mammals, and what this means practically for people who are back or stomach sleepers

Nadi shodhana, or alternate nostril breathing, is a pranayama practice from the Hatha yoga tradition in which the practitioner uses the thumb and ring finger to close one nostril at a time, breathing in through one side and out through the other in a structured alternating pattern; explain what nadi shodhana is and what the traditional theory of the ida and pingala energy channels claims it does, what the modern physiological research shows about the relationship between nasal dominance and brain hemisphere activity, and why studies on alternate nostril breathing show measurable effects on blood pressure and sympathetic nervous system tone

he glymphatic system, which clears toxic waste from the brain during sleep, is approximately sixty percent more active when you sleep on your side than on your back — a finding from research at the University of Rochester that used MRI to track cerebrospinal fluid flow in different sleep positions; explain what sleep position has to do with brain waste clearance, why the lateral position appears to be the most efficient for glymphatic drainage, what researchers speculate about the evolutionary origins of side sleeping as the dominant position across most mammals, and what this means practically for people who are back or stomach sleepers
